beats.title: Kore cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I want to stress-test the observable-versus-vague classifier on my own rubric, because I suspect some criteria are secretly hybrids. Take my ten-criterion essay rubric and (1) classify each line as observable or vague, and for the vague ones show me the rewritten observable behavior in your amber-versus-observable diff format; (2) generate one top-scoring submission against the vague version and one against the rewritten version so I can compare what each actually elicits; (3) flag any rewritten criterion a motivated student could still game without doing the real thinking.

beats.narration_text: Humanitarians AI. Brutalist and Educational AI. Build it with Claude — then take it apart.

props.command: Build a Rubric Adjective Detector with Claude
props.segment: Build a Rubric Adjective Detector with Claude

beats.narration_text: The rubrics most teachers use are written in a language Claude can game perfectly — because the criteria are aesthetic adjectives, not observable behaviors. 'Demonstrates strong analytical thinking' is not a criterion. It is a verdict. A student reading it learns nothing about what to change.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: rubric cell showing 'Analysis: Good. Demonstrates strong analytical thinking.' Amber highlight on the quality adjective. No CLI yet — this is the problem.

beats.narration_text: Pipe the rubric into Claude. Ask it to classify each criterion as observable — can be verified without evaluator judgment — or vague, which requires taste. For each vague criterion, rewrite it as a specific, observable behavior.

props.command: claude "Read this rubric and classify each criterion: observable (can be verified without evaluator judgment) or vague (requires taste/interpretation). For each vague criterion, rewrite it as a specific, observable behavior a student can demonstrate and an evaluator can check. Format: ORIGINAL | TYPE | REWRITTEN." < rubric.md

beats.narration_text: Read the Python script before trusting it. It pipes rubric.md to Claude, parses the three-column output — original, type, rewritten — and prints a formatted diff. Vague criteria on the left in amber, observable rewrites on the right.

props.code:
import subprocess, sys
from pathlib import Path

def audit_rubric(rubric_file):
    prompt = (
        "Classify each criterion: observable or vague.\n"
        "For each vague, rewrite as observable behavior.\n"
        "Format: ORIGINAL | TYPE | REWRITTEN"
    )
    text = Path(rubric_file).read_text()
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["claude", prompt],
        input=text, capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    for line in result.stdout.splitlines():
        parts = line.split(" | ")
        if len(parts) == 3:
            orig, typ, rewrite = parts
            tag = "[VAGUE]" if "vague" in typ.lower() else "[OK]"
            print(f"{tag} {orig}\n  -> {rewrite}\n")
props.title: rubric_audit.py

beats.narration_text: The output shows the classification. 'Demonstrates sophisticated analysis' — vague. Rewrite: 'Each body paragraph connects at least one piece of evidence directly to the paragraph's claim. The explanation interprets the evidence — it does not restate it.' That is observable. A student can self-assess before submitting.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: two-column before/after. Left: 'Demonstrates sophisticated analysis' in amber. Right: the observable rewrite in clean black text. Label below: 'can self-assess before submitting'.

beats.narration_text: The change: pipe the rewritten observable rubric back through Claude and ask it to produce a top-scoring student submission. Run both the vague rubric and the observable rubric through the same prompt — and compare the submissions.

props.command: claude "Here is a rubric. Write a student essay that would score at the top performance level on every criterion. Be precise about which rubric language you are satisfying." < rewritten_rubric.md

beats.narration_text: The vague rubric produces an essay that sounds insightful — 'demonstrates sophisticated analysis of the author's purpose.' The observable rubric produces an essay that actually contains what the criterion demands: a claim in the first paragraph, two supporting examples with citations, interpretation that does not restate the evidence. The vague rubric didn't fail because Claude misunderstood it. It succeeded because Claude understood it perfectly.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: side-by-side submissions. Vague rubric output: generic academic prose. Observable rubric output: specific structural elements highlighted — claim statement, two examples, non-restating interpretation.

beats.narration_text: Vague rubric criteria don't fail because they are unclear to humans. They fail because they are too clear to AI. A model knows exactly how to produce output that sounds insightful without being insightful. Observable criteria close that gap — not by making the rubric harder, but by making it specific enough to mean something.
beats.visual_intent: Slate: teardown summary. 'Vague = AI-gameable. Observable = verifiable.' The distinction stated once, clean.
